Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional. I'd dedicate about 18 hours a week to studying, but I preferred working in longer, focused blocks rather than spreading it out evenly. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time creating my own summaries and notes from the materials. I found that working through things on paper or in Word documents helped me process information better than just clicking through online content.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied quite a bit depending on what else was going on in my life. I have extracurricular commitments that sometimes took priority, so I'd adjust my study schedule around those. When deadlines approached, I'd intensify my efforts, but otherwise I maintained a flexible approach that worked with my high resilience and ability to bounce back when needed.
Q3: I usually logged in when I needed to check for new announcements or download materials I hadn't grabbed yet. Sometimes I'd log in to participate in discussions when I felt I had something meaningful to contribute. Honestly, I didn't feel the need to be on there constantly since I did most of my actual studying offline with downloaded resources.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. I'd go in spurts—downloading everything I needed, maybe watching some videos, then disappearing for a while to actually process the material. My routine was more about deep engagement with the content itself rather than frequent platform visits.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me—I watched over three hours worth because they helped me understand complex concepts before I translated them into my own notes. The discussion forums were also useful because connecting with peers gave me different perspectives on the material. I appreciated resources I could download and annotate at my own pace.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't complete many of the smaller tasks or activities that felt like busywork. If something didn't directly contribute to my understanding or exam preparation, I'd skip it. My assignment completion rate reflects this—I focused on quality over quantity, prioritizing what would actually help me learn deeply rather than just checking boxes.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when it challenged me intellectually or filled a gap in my understanding. I valued things that I could engage with actively—writing, analyzing, discussing—rather than passive consumption. If I could see how something connected to the bigger picture or would help me on exams, I was all in.
Q8: Absolutely, they felt different. Required activities had my attention because they directly impacted my grade, but optional ones I evaluated more critically. I'd only engage with optional content if it genuinely interested me or seemed like it would deepen my understanding. I didn't do things just for the sake of appearing active.
Q9: Oh, constantly. I downloaded PDFs, lecture slides, and readings to study offline. I used textbooks from the library, created extensive handwritten notes, and even found supplementary materials online. My actual learning happened mostly away from the LMS—at my desk, in coffee shops, wherever I could focus without distractions.
Q10: Most of my studying wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours rewriting concepts in my own words, creating flashcards, and working through practice problems on paper. I also studied with a couple of peers in person, which was incredibly helpful but left no digital trace. My exam score reflects this invisible work.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd download a video or document once, then study it multiple times offline over days or even weeks. The LMS would only show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times. It's just more efficient for me to work with local files.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, particularly during weeks when my stress levels peaked. Even though I generally handle pressure well, there were times when competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars made it difficult to engage consistently. I'd feel surprised by how quickly time passed without logging in.
Q13: Yes, definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed with other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect because I knew I could catch up later. I'd prioritize my offline studying over platform engagement since that's where my real learning happened. Competing responsibilities definitely shaped my usage patterns.
Q14: Sometimes I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything—just going through the motions. This usually happened when I was tired or stressed. I'd realize I'd watched a video but couldn't recall what it covered, so I'd have to revisit it later when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I postponed quite a few assignments, honestly. Even knowing they were important, I'd prioritize exam preparation over smaller tasks because I knew the exam carried more weight. My assignment completion rate suffered, but my exam performance shows where I directed my energy. It was a calculated trade-off.
Q16: Deadlines created bursts of activity for me. I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS right before something was due, downloading materials and checking requirements. But between deadlines, my presence was minimal. This pattern worked for me even if it looked inconsistent from the outside.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was constructive and specific. Positive feedback energized me to engage more deeply, while critical feedback made me revisit materials to address gaps. The peer support intervention was actually really helpful—having others encourage me made a noticeable difference in my mood and motivation.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I appreciated, but it also meant I had to be self-directed. I thrived with that autonomy because of my high resilience, but I can see how it might not work for everyone. The clear exam structure helped me prioritize what really mattered.
Q19: Having downloadable resources made engagement easier because I could study anywhere. However, the emphasis on frequent small tasks made engagement harder for me—I preferred deeper, less frequent engagement. The discussion forums were well-designed and made peer interaction feel natural and valuable.
Q20: I think those students might be exploring, searching for what they need, or struggling to find the right resources. They could also be perfectionists who review everything multiple times before feeling ready to submit. Or maybe they're dealing with anxiety about their work not being good enough.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My low LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, my notes, and offline study sessions. I earned a Distinction despite minimal platform engagement because the LMS was just a portal to resources, not where my learning actually happened.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and submission timing, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior. But they completely miss the depth of engagement, offline study, peer discussions that happen in person, and the quality of learning. They capture clicks, not comprehension.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, questioning concepts, and connecting ideas to what I already know. It's about intellectual investment, not just showing up. I feel engaged when I'm curious, when I want to understand deeply, not just pass. It's an internal state that doesn't always manifest in visible activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline studying, my handwritten notes, the peer study sessions, or the hours I spent thinking through problems while walking or doing other things. It also wouldn't capture my emotional journey—the surprise at how much I enjoyed certain topics, or my resilience in bouncing back from stressful periods. My real engagement was largely invisible to the platform.
